label: The Migration Period
pitch:
When The Huns Enter Europe around 375, they set a continent in motion. The Crossing of the Rhine on the last night of 406 sent Vandals, Suebi, and Alans pouring into Gaul; within a generation Geiseric Leads the Vandals into Africa and seized Carthage, and in 455 The Vandals Sack Rome. Attila Invades Gaul and was barely stopped at the Catalaunian Plains, and in 476 Romulus Augustulus Deposed ended the Western Western Empire.

This is the Völkerwanderung — the great shattering. Out of it came the successor kingdoms that became Europe: the Franks under Clovis, the Burgundians, the Lombards who took Italy in 568, and a Roman world that dissolved into a patchwork of barbarian states even as the Plague of Justinian and the climate disaster of 536 hammered what remained.

spans.label: The Hunnic Trigger
spans.description: The Huns' arrival on the Pontic steppe drove the Goths into the empire and built a short-lived steppe empire that peaked under Attila.

spans.label: The Crossing of the Rhine
spans.description: Vandals, Suebi, and Alans broke across the frozen Rhine and moved through Gaul and Hispania toward North Africa.

spans.label: The Vandal Kingdom of Carthage
spans.description: The Vandals built a naval kingdom in North Africa that controlled the western Mediterranean and sacked Rome in 455.

spans.label: The Fall of the Western Empire
spans.description: A rapid succession of puppet emperors ended in 476 when Odoacer deposed the last Western emperor and ruled Italy as king.

spans.label: The Successor Kingdoms
spans.description: Frankish, Burgundian, Visigothic, and Ostrogothic kingdoms replaced Roman administration across the former Western provinces.

spans.label: Justinian's Reconquest
spans.description: Emperor Justinian sent Belisarius and Narses to reclaim North Africa, Italy, and parts of Spain from Germanic successor kingdoms.

spans.label: The Lombard Conquest of Italy
spans.description: The Lombards invaded a war-ruined Italy and seized much of the peninsula, the last great migration into the West.

events.label: The Huns Enter Europe
events.description: Mounted nomads from the eastern steppe overran the Goths and triggered the chain of migrations that ended the Western Empire.

events.label: Hunnic Society and Warfare
events.description: The Huns were a confederation of horse nomads whose composite bows, mobile tactics, and lack of fixed settlements made them unlike any enemy Rome had faced. They herded cattle, extracted tribute, and incorporated defeated peoples into their ranks.

events.label: Gothic Refugees Flood the Empire
events.description: Tens of thousands of Goths crossed the Danube fleeing the Huns, the first wave of peoples pushed into Roman territory.

events.label: Battle of Adrianople
events.description: Gothic cavalry shattered a Roman army and killed Emperor Valens, the greatest Roman military defeat in centuries. The disaster forced Rome to accept the Goths inside the empire as armed settlers rather than subjects.

events.label: Theodosius Makes Peace with the Goths
events.description: Emperor Theodosius I settled the Goths inside the Balkans as federates, granting them land and autonomy in exchange for military service. The arrangement set a template for accommodating barbarian peoples that Rome would use — with increasing desperation — for the next century.

events.label: The Huns Shatter the Greuthungi Goths
events.description: The Hunnic advance first overwhelmed the Greuthungi (later called Ostrogoths) on the Pontic steppe, then drove the Tervingi (later called Visigoths) toward the Danube frontier.

events.label: Stilicho Commands the Western Army
events.description: The half-Vandal general Stilicho became the dominant military figure in the Western Empire, holding together a disintegrating frontier through diplomacy, bribery, and hard fighting. His execution in 408 removed the West's last effective generalissimo.

events.label: Alaric Besieges Rome
events.description: The Visigothic leader Alaric blockaded Rome twice before sacking it in 410, using the city as leverage to extract land and military titles from a court that repeatedly broke its promises.

events.label: The Visigoths Sack Rome
events.description: Alaric's Visigoths entered Rome and plundered it for three days — the first time in eight centuries the city had been taken by an enemy. The psychological shock resounded across the Roman world.

events.label: Radagaisus Invades Italy
events.description: A large Gothic war band under Radagaisus crossed the Danube and pushed deep into Italy before Stilicho defeated and executed him near Florence. The episode stripped frontier garrisons and set conditions for the Rhine crossing the following year.

events.label: The Crossing of the Rhine
events.description: On the last night of 406 a coalition of Vandals, Suebi, and Alans crossed the frozen Rhine, breaking the western frontier for good.

events.label: Vandals, Suebi, and Alans Enter Gaul
events.description: The Rhine peoples ravaged Gaul for three years before moving on toward the Pyrenees and Hispania.

events.label: Constantine III's Usurpation
events.description: A soldier proclaimed emperor in Britain stripped the island and Gaul of troops, accelerating the western collapse.

events.label: Rome Abandons Britain
events.description: The emperor Honorius told the British cities to look to their own defense, ending four centuries of Roman rule on the island.

events.label: The Burgundian Kingdom on the Rhine
events.description: The Burgundians settled a kingdom on the middle Rhine, later remembered in the Nibelung legends.

events.label: Visigoths Settled in Aquitaine
events.description: The Western court granted the Visigoths federate territory in southwestern Gaul centered on Toulouse, the first formal barbarian kingdom carved out of Roman provincial land. The settlement was in exchange for military service against other barbarians.

events.label: The Vandals Cross into Hispania
events.description: Pushed by Rome and the Visigoths, the Vandals and their allies moved into the Iberian Peninsula.

events.label: The Suebi Kingdom in Gallaecia
events.description: The Suebi settled the northwest corner of Hispania (modern Galicia and northern Portugal) and built the longest-lasting of the Rhine-crossing peoples' kingdoms, surviving until the Visigoths absorbed them in 585.

events.label: The Alans Disperse into Hispania and Africa
events.description: The Alan people, originally steppe-dwellers from the Caucasus region, split after heavy losses in Hispania: some joined the Vandals crossing into Africa, others were absorbed by neighboring peoples. The Alans effectively ceased to exist as a distinct group.

events.label: The Federate System Shapes the West
events.description: Rome increasingly relied on barbarian federates — peoples settled on imperial land in exchange for military service — to defend frontiers it could no longer garrison. The system gradually transferred real power from the imperial court to barbarian commanders.

events.label: Geiseric Leads the Vandals into Africa
events.description: The Vandal king Geiseric ferried his people to North Africa, the empire's richest grain province.

events.label: Siege of Hippo Regius
events.description: The Vandals besieged Hippo Regius on the North African coast; the bishop Augustine of Hippo died during the siege. The city fell to the Vandals after a long blockade, stripping the empire of another major port.

events.label: The Vandals Take Carthage
events.description: Geiseric seized Carthage and built a Mediterranean naval power that throttled Rome's grain and tax base.

events.label: The Vandal Navy Dominates the Western Mediterranean
events.description: Geiseric built a fleet that raided Sicily, Sardinia, and the Italian coastline, demonstrating that the Vandals had become the supreme naval power in the western sea. Rome lost control of its critical sea-lanes.

events.label: Attila Becomes King of the Huns
events.description: Attila built the Hunnic confederation into the terror of two empires, extracting tribute from Constantinople.

events.label: Attila Kills His Brother Bleda
events.description: Attila murdered his co-ruler brother Bleda, becoming sole king of the Huns and concentrating all power in his hands. The killing removed the last internal check on his ambitions.

events.label: Attila Extracts Tribute from Constantinople
events.description: After devastating raids through the Balkans, Attila extracted a large annual gold tribute from the Eastern Empire in the peace of Anatolius. Constantinople paid to avoid further destruction.

events.label: The Hunnic Court at the Theiss
events.description: Described by the Roman envoy Priscus, Attila's court on the Hungarian plain combined steppe customs with Roman luxuries: wooden halls, silk-clad servants, gold and silver tableware, and a ruler who ostentatiously ate from a wooden platter to emphasize his warrior simplicity.

events.label: Attila Invades Gaul
events.description: Attila led a vast army into Gaul, sacking cities until a Roman-Visigothic coalition confronted him.

events.label: Battle of the Catalaunian Plains
events.description: Aetius and the Visigoths halted Attila in one of late antiquity's largest battles, his only major defeat.

events.label: Theodoric I Dies at the Catalaunian Plains
events.description: The Visigothic king Theodoric I was killed during the battle that bore his coalition's name as the moment of victory. His death was celebrated in Visigothic legend and demonstrates the scale of the fighting.

events.label: Attila Invades Italy
events.description: Attila ravaged northern Italy before withdrawing after a famous meeting with Pope Leo I.

events.label: Pope Leo I Meets Attila
events.description: Pope Leo I led a delegation that met Attila on the Mincio River; Attila withdrew from Italy shortly after, though disease and supply shortage likely played as large a role as the meeting. The encounter became a founding legend of papal political authority.

events.label: Attila Dies; the Hunnic Empire Collapses
events.description: Attila's sudden death and the Battle of Nedao shattered the Hunnic confederation within a year.

events.label: Battle of Nedao
events.description: Subject Germanic peoples destroyed the Huns' army and freed themselves, redrawing the map of central Europe.

events.label: Aetius: the Last of the Romans
events.description: Flavius Aetius was the dominant military commander of the Western Empire in the 430s–450s, skillfully playing barbarian groups against one another to maintain a semblance of Roman control. His murder by Emperor Valentinian III in 454 removed the West's last capable strategist.

events.label: Valentinian III Murdered
events.description: Emperor Valentinian III was assassinated by loyalists of the general Aetius whom he had killed the year before. His death opened the floodgates to a series of short-lived emperors and the Vandal sack.

events.label: The Vandals Sack Rome
events.description: Geiseric's Vandals plundered Rome for two weeks, the second sack of the city in half a century.

events.label: The Shadow Emperors of the West
events.description: Between 455 and 476 the Western throne cycled through nine emperors in twenty-one years, most of them installed and discarded by Germanic warlords. Real power lay with military commanders, not the purple-clad figures on the throne.

events.label: Ricimer Controls the Western Court
events.description: The half-Visigothic general Ricimer became the kingmaker of the Western Empire, making and unmaking emperors from 456 to 472 while himself barred from the throne by his barbarian blood. He exemplified the paradox of the late Western Empire: real power in barbarian hands, symbolic authority remaining Roman.

events.label: Majorian's Attempted Recovery
events.description: Emperor Majorian undertook the most ambitious Western recovery effort of the fifth century, reforming taxation, rebuilding the fleet, and campaigning in Gaul and Hispania. His planned assault on the Vandals was betrayed; Ricimer had him executed in 461.

events.label: The Domain of Soissons
events.description: A rump Roman state in northern Gaul under Syagrius outlived the Western Empire by a decade.

events.label: Romulus Augustulus Deposed
events.description: Odoacer deposed the boy-emperor Romulus Augustulus, the conventional end date of the Western Roman Empire.

events.label: Odoacer Rules Italy
events.description: The Germanic commander Odoacer ruled Italy as king while nominally recognizing the Eastern emperor.

events.label: Julius Nepos: the Last Legitimate Western Emperor
events.description: Julius Nepos, recognized by Constantinople as the legitimate Western emperor, was deposed and exiled to Dalmatia in 475. He remained alive — and technically emperor — until his assassination in 480, meaning the Western Empire's legal extinction was even messier than the 476 narrative suggests.

events.label: The Collapse of the Grain Fleet
events.description: The Vandal seizure of Africa ended the great state grain shipments that had fed Rome, unravelling the Mediterranean economy.

events.label: Cities Shrink Across the West
events.description: Archaeological evidence shows dramatic reductions in city populations, long-distance trade goods, and monumental construction across the western provinces during the fifth century. Rural villas were abandoned, coin use declined, and the ceramic and glass industries that had linked the Roman world contracted sharply.

events.label: Clovis Unites the Franks
events.description: Clovis I welded the Frankish groups into a single kingdom that would become the dominant power in the West.

events.label: Battle of Soissons
events.description: Clovis destroyed the last Roman domain in Gaul, opening the way to Frankish rule.

events.label: The Baptism of Clovis
events.description: Clovis converted directly to Catholic Christianity, aligning the Franks with the Roman population they ruled.

events.label: Battle of Vouillé
events.description: Clovis defeated and killed the Visigothic king Alaric II at Vouillé, driving the Visigoths out of Gaul and confining them to Hispania. The battle established the Franks as the dominant power in the former western provinces.

events.label: The Lex Salica Codified
events.description: Clovis commissioned the written codification of Frankish customary law as the Lex Salica, creating a legal text that governed the Frankish kingdom and influenced European law for centuries.

events.label: Gundobad and Burgundian Law
events.description: The Burgundian king Gundobad issued the Lex Romana Burgundionum (Burgundian law for Romans) alongside a separate barbarian code, managing the coexistence of his Burgundian and Gallo-Roman subjects. The dual-code approach was characteristic of the successor kingdoms.

events.label: The Breviary of Alaric
events.description: The Visigothic king Alaric II issued a simplified digest of Roman law for his Roman subjects, preserving a body of late Roman legal practice in the West long after the empire's collapse.

events.label: Theodoric the Great Conquers Italy
events.description: The Ostrogothic king Theodoric, sent by Constantinople to resolve the Odoacer problem, defeated and killed Odoacer and established the Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy. Theodoric ruled as an effective overlord of multiple Germanic kingdoms, presenting himself as the legitimate heir of Roman administration.

events.label: Theodoric as Overlord of the West
events.description: At his peak Theodoric held Italy and controlled the Visigoths through his daughter, the Burgundians through marriage alliance, and the Alamans through protection. His Ravenna court employed Roman administrators like Cassiodorus and the philosopher Boethius.

events.label: Boethius and the Consolation of Philosophy
events.description: The philosopher and Roman aristocrat Boethius was imprisoned and executed by Theodoric on charges of treason. He wrote the Consolation of Philosophy while awaiting death — a meditation on fortune and reason that became one of the most widely read books of the Middle Ages.

events.label: Cassiodorus Preserves Roman Administration
events.description: The Roman official Cassiodorus served as Theodoric's chief minister, writing official correspondence and later founding a monastery at Vivarium dedicated to preserving classical and Christian texts. His work bridged the Roman and medieval worlds.

events.label: The Arian-Catholic Fault Line in Successor Kingdoms
events.description: Most Germanic ruling classes were Arian Christians — following the theology condemned at Nicaea — while their Roman subjects were Catholic. This difference in faith created a persistent political and social divide in the Visigothic, Vandal, Burgundian, and Ostrogothic kingdoms.

events.label: The Arian-to-Catholic Conversions
events.description: Over the sixth century the Germanic kingdoms abandoned Arianism for Catholicism, fusing ruler and ruled.

events.label: The Frankish Church Alliance
events.description: By converting to Catholic Christianity rather than Arianism, Clovis gained the active support of the Gallo-Roman bishops, the most powerful local administrators surviving from the Roman period. The alliance between the Frankish crown and the Catholic Church became the defining feature of medieval European politics.

events.label: The Vandals Persecute Catholic Clergy
events.description: The Vandal king Huneric exiled hundreds of Catholic bishops and clergy from Africa, attempting to impose Arianism on the conquered Roman population. The persecution alienated the Roman population and left a legacy that helped justify Justinian's later reconquest as religious liberation.

events.label: The Franks Absorb Burgundy and Provence
events.description: The sons of Clovis swallowed the Burgundian kingdom and extended Frankish power to the Mediterranean.

events.label: Justinian Declares the Reconquest
events.description: Emperor Justinian I came to power with a vision of restoring the united Roman Empire. Working with his formidable wife Theodora and a circle of brilliant administrators, he launched legal reforms and military campaigns to reclaim the western provinces.

events.label: The Corpus Juris Civilis
events.description: Justinian's legal commission produced the Corpus Juris Civilis — a systematic compilation of Roman law that remains the basis of legal systems across continental Europe and Latin America. It was the most consequential legislative act of late antiquity.

events.label: Belisarius Lands in North Africa
events.description: Justinian's general Belisarius sailed to North Africa with a relatively small but professional force. He won two rapid battles against the Vandal king Gelimer and entered Carthage within months of landing.

events.label: Belisarius Destroys the Vandal Kingdom
events.description: Justinian's general Belisarius reconquered North Africa in a lightning campaign, ending the Vandal state.

events.label: The Vandal Kingdom Extinguished
events.description: Gelimer, the last Vandal king, surrendered to Belisarius and walked in a triumph through Constantinople. The Vandal people were dispersed into the Byzantine army; the Vandal ruling class effectively ceased to exist as a political entity.

events.label: The Gothic War Begins in Italy
events.description: After deposing Theodoric's successors, Justinian sent Belisarius to reconquer Italy from the Ostrogoths. The war dragged on for nearly two decades and devastated the Italian peninsula far more than any barbarian raid had.

events.label: Belisarius Takes and Holds Rome
events.description: Belisarius entered Rome in 536 and then defended it against Ostrogothic siege for more than a year — one of the most remarkable defensive operations of late antiquity. The city changed hands multiple times during the war.

events.label: Narses Ends the Gothic War
events.description: The eunuch general Narses finished what Belisarius had started, defeating the Ostrogothic king Totila at the Battle of Busta Gallorum and then crushing the remnants under Teia. Italy was back under Byzantine control — ruined, depopulated, and exhausted.

events.label: The Pragmatic Sanction
events.description: Justinian's Pragmatic Sanction re-established imperial law across Italy, formally ending the Ostrogothic kingdom. It attempted to restore Roman administration to a peninsula that had been ravaged by nearly twenty years of continuous warfare.

events.label: The Lazic War Distracts Justinian
events.description: Conflict with Persia over the Lazic kingdom on the eastern Black Sea coastline forced Justinian to fight on two fronts simultaneously, diverting resources from the Italian campaign and prolonging the Gothic War.

events.label: The Human Cost of the Gothic War
events.description: Contemporary accounts describe Italian cities depopulated, farmland reverting to swamp, and populations starved during the Gothic War sieges. The war that was supposed to restore Roman civilization to Italy did more lasting damage to it than the migrations themselves.

events.label: The Dust Veil of 536
events.description: A volcanic catastrophe dimmed the sun for over a year, causing crop failure and famine across the post-Roman world.

events.label: Tree Ring Evidence of 536 Climate Collapse
events.description: Ice cores and tree rings from across the Northern Hemisphere record a dramatic cooling event beginning in 536, now linked to a massive volcanic eruption. The resulting agricultural failures compounded the social disruptions of the migration era.

events.label: The Plague of Justinian
events.description: The first recorded bubonic plague pandemic killed a vast share of the Mediterranean population.

events.label: The Plague Spreads to the West
events.description: The Plague of Justinian reached Constantinople in 541 and spread rapidly along trade routes to Egypt, North Africa, Gaul, and the British Isles. It returned in multiple waves through the late sixth and seventh centuries, fundamentally reshaping Mediterranean demographics.

events.label: The Lombards in Pannonia
events.description: The Lombards, originally from Scandinavia, had migrated south over centuries and settled in Pannonia (modern Hungary) as Byzantine allies. They absorbed Gepid and other peoples and built up strength for their eventual Italian invasion.

events.label: The Lombards Invade Italy
events.description: The Lombards poured into a Byzantine-ruined Italy, the last of the great Germanic migrations into the West.

events.label: Alboin Leads the Lombards
events.description: King Alboin led the Lombard people from Pannonia into Italy, founding a kingdom centered on Pavia.

events.label: Pavia Becomes the Lombard Capital
events.description: After a three-year siege, the Lombards captured Pavia (ancient Ticinum) and made it their royal capital. The Lombard kingdom that emerged divided Italy into a patchwork of Lombard-held territories and Byzantine exarchates that persisted for two centuries.

events.label: The Post-Roman West Takes Shape
events.description: By the late sixth century the Western provinces had become a settled map of Germanic kingdoms, the foundation of medieval Europe.

events.label: The Successor-Kingdom Law Codes
events.description: Germanic kings issued written codes such as the Lex Romana Visigothorum to govern their Roman and barbarian subjects.

events.label: The Decline of the Roman Villa Economy
events.description: The great Roman villa estates that had dominated the western countryside shrank, were abandoned, or changed hands to barbarian landlords. The shift from market-oriented agriculture to subsistence farming and local self-sufficiency marked the end of the integrated Roman rural economy.

events.label: The Church as Administrative Continuity
events.description: As imperial administration collapsed in the West, bishops assumed civic functions: negotiating with barbarian kings, organizing food distribution, maintaining aqueducts, and preserving Latin literacy. The Church became the primary institution of administrative continuity between the Roman and medieval worlds.

events.label: Priscus's Embassy to Attila
events.description: The rhetorician Priscus accompanied a Roman diplomatic mission to Attila's camp and recorded detailed observations of Hunnic life, court ceremony, and Attila's personality. His account is the most vivid primary source from the migration period.

events.label: Burgundian Law Distinguishes Romans and Burgundians
events.description: The Burgundian law codes maintained separate legal procedures for Romans and Burgundians, reflecting the reality that successor kingdoms governed ethnically mixed populations. Over time the distinctions blurred as the two groups intermarried and merged.

events.label: The Mosaics of Ravenna
events.description: The imperial capital at Ravenna — seat of the Western court since 402 — was adorned by Theodoric and his predecessors with some of the finest late-antique mosaics in existence. The buildings survive as a unique record of a world where Roman, Germanic, and Byzantine cultures briefly coexisted.

events.label: The Visigothic Kingdom Consolidates in Spain
events.description: After losing Gaul to the Franks at Vouillé, the Visigoths consolidated in the Iberian Peninsula with their capital at Toledo. The kingdom survived for nearly two centuries, becoming one of the most culturally productive of the successor states.

events.label: The Variae of Cassiodorus
events.description: Cassiodorus collected his official correspondence as chief minister of the Ostrogothic court into the Variae, a compendium that reveals how Theodoric's administration attempted to blend Roman legal and administrative practice with Gothic rule.

events.label: Justinian Builds the Hagia Sophia
events.description: Justinian's rebuild of the Hagia Sophia in Constantinople produced the largest domed interior in the ancient world and remains one of humanity's greatest architectural achievements. Its construction demonstrated that even as the West fragmented, the Eastern Empire retained its wealth and ambition.

events.label: The Edictum Rothari
events.description: The Lombard king Rothari later codified Lombard customary law in the Edictum Rothari (643), the first written Lombard law code — part of a broader wave of Germanic law-writing that preserved barbarian custom in Latin form.

events.label: Migration-Period Metalwork and Art
events.description: The migration period produced a distinctive artistic style characterized by interlaced animal ornament on belt fittings, brooches, and weapons. These portable luxury objects traveled with peoples across the continent and are the primary archaeological evidence for the migrations.

events.label: Theodora, Co-ruler of the East
events.description: Justinian's wife Theodora exercised real political power as co-ruler, reportedly stiffening Justinian's resolve during the Nika Riots and influencing religious and social policy. She represents the outsized role of capable individuals at the Byzantine court.

events.label: The Nika Riots
events.description: A mass uprising in Constantinople — the Nika Riots — nearly toppled Justinian before imperial troops suppressed it at a cost of tens of thousands of lives. The crisis demonstrated the volatility of the Eastern capital even as Justinian prepared his western campaigns.

events.label: The Visigothic Conversion to Catholicism
events.description: King Reccared of the Visigoths formally converted from Arianism to Catholicism at the Third Council of Toledo, ending the religious divide between the Germanic rulers and their Roman subjects in Spain. The conversion exemplified the broader process by which the successor kingdoms fused into a new European Christian civilization.

events.label: Germanic Kingship Replaces Roman Office
events.description: Across the former Western provinces, the warrior king who distributed land and treasure replaced the Roman magistrate who administered law and collected taxes. The new rulers needed different kinds of legitimacy — military prowess, Christian piety, and dynastic descent rather than imperial appointment.

events.label: Western Monasticism Expands
events.description: Benedict of Nursia founded Monte Cassino and wrote the Rule of Saint Benedict, providing a template for monastic organization that spread throughout the West. Monasteries became the principal centers of literacy, agriculture, and social welfare in the post-Roman countryside.

events.label: Hunnic Terror Tactics Reshape Warfare
events.description: The Hunnic practice of skull-binding infants to elongate heads and their use of swift composite-bow cavalry were observed with horror and fascination by Roman writers. Their tactics forced Roman armies to adapt, and their arrival created a chain reaction of displacement that spread across the entire continent.

events.label: Romanized Barbarians Inside the Army
events.description: By the late fourth century large portions of the Roman army consisted of Germanic soldiers, many of them rising to high command. Generals like Stilicho, Aetius (raised partly among the Huns), and Ricimer were products of a world where Roman and barbarian identities overlapped.

events.label: The Hunnic Disruption of Trade Routes
events.description: Hunnic dominance of the Pontic steppe disrupted the overland trade connections between the Roman world and Central Asia, affecting the supply of silk and other eastern luxuries. The disruption pushed more trade onto sea routes and shifted economic geography.

events.label: The Vandals Control North African Grain
events.description: By controlling North Africa, Geiseric held the granary that had fed Rome and the western armies for centuries. The loss of African grain and tax revenue accelerated the fiscal collapse of the Western Empire and forced it to reduce the army further.

events.label: Goths Split into Visigoths and Ostrogoths
events.description: The death of Theodosius I and the division of the empire between his sons formalized the split between the Gothic groups: the Visigoths under Alaric headed west, while the Ostrogoths remained tied to the Hunnic sphere until Attila's death freed them.

events.label: Clovis Defeats the Alemanni
events.description: Clovis's victory over the Alemanni at Tolbiac (traditionally associated with his conversion vow) consolidated Frankish control over the upper Rhine. The battle removed a rival Germanic power from the region that would become Alsace and Switzerland.

events.label: Frankish Church Councils Shape Law
events.description: Clovis convened the Council of Orléans in 511, the first of many Frankish church councils that legislated jointly on ecclesiastical and secular matters. The councils illustrate the deep partnership between Frankish kingship and Catholic Christianity.

events.label: Population Decline in the Western Provinces
events.description: The combination of warfare, plague, famine from climate disruption, and the collapse of the complex Roman economy caused significant population decline across the western provinces. Some regions, particularly in Gaul and Britain, may not have recovered their Roman-era populations for centuries.

events.label: Vandal Court Culture in Carthage
events.description: The Vandal court at Carthage patronized Latin poets and maintained many elements of Roman elite culture — baths, games, and literary culture — even while enforcing Arian Christianity and persecuting Catholic clergy. The Vandals were simultaneously the destroyers and inheritors of Roman civilization.

events.label: The Lombard Duchy System
events.description: Unlike the Ostrogoths, who had maintained a centralized administration, the Lombards governed Italy through a system of largely autonomous dukes. The fragmentation of authority made the Lombard kingdom politically unstable but gave the Church and local Roman elites more room to operate.
